result:
The first thing I notice is the hum.  
Not sound, exactly—more the way a neon tube vibrates at the edge of vision, a muscle memory of light. It is 3:07 a.m. and the city is practicing a kind of sleep that resembles insomnia: eyes closed, pulse still public. I’m walking across the pedestrian bridge that used to smell of urine and roasted peanuts; tonight it smells of neither, only ozone after rain and something metallic, like a coin held too long in the palm. Below, the river is a black ribbon somebody forgot to cut. Every thirty seconds a car hisses underneath, a match struck and instantly blown out.  

I carry a notebook I will never open. The pages are wedged so tight with half-started sentences they have achieved the density of wood; the book has become a club, a weapon against the future. If I threw it over the railing it would sink, I think, without a splash—would become a dark rectangular fish, swimming off to file its own report.  

Instead I keep walking, because motion is the only prescription I can still afford. The doctor said: “Try to inhabit the front edge of your body.” I pictured a coastline, cliffs wearing away in real time, every grain of salt a second I would never get back.  

On the far side of the bridge a convenience store glows like a vending machine for lost time. Inside, a boy in a green apron is rehearsing a conversation with his ex-girlfriend, moving his lips while he arranges the energy drinks into a fortress. I buy nothing, but the fluorescent light gifts me a moment of autobiography: I see myself at twelve, stealing foil-wrapped sticks of juicy fruit, the shame as bright and sweet as the gum itself. The boy’s badge says “Trainee.” I want to tell him that everyone is training for a war that ended before we enlisted, but instead I nod and leave, the door jingling like a joke nobody gets.  

Back outside, the air has thickened. A single abandoned umbrella hangs from the railing like a bat. I take it down, open it; the fabric is ripped into star shapes. Underneath, I am suddenly a private planet, protected from a sky that has forgotten how to rain. I aim myself toward the old printing district where my father worked when printers still bled ink. The buildings there have been gutted, turned into apartments the size of envelopes. Still, the smell of linseed lingers in the bricks like a family secret.  

I find the courtyard where he used to smoke during breaks. Someone has installed a sculpture: two steel wings welded to a bicycle frame, half thrust into the ground. A plaque reads “Ascending Productivity.” I sit on the base and talk to him, though he has been dead for eleven years.  
“Dad, the world ended,” I say, “but they kept publishing the newspaper, just smaller.”  
The sculpture gives no answer, but one wing creaks, a small metallic sigh that might be amusement or arthritis.  

A drone passes overhead, red eye blinking. It maps the thermal signature of my grief, uploads it to a server farm cooled by river water. I wave. Somewhere a technician blinks at a screen, sees a yellow-orange ghost shaped like a middle-aged child. He tags it “ambient wildlife” and moves on.  

Time dilates. I remember that as a boy I believed night was a blanket God pulled over the world so we could practice dying in private. Then morning was the ripped seam, light pouring through the wound. Now I think morning is just night’s hangover, a pale apology nobody asked for.  

My phone vibrates: a message from a woman I kissed once in a museum among marble limbs. She writes: “The exhibition closed. They packed the statues first, heads wrapped in polyethylene like produce. I thought of you.” I type three replies, delete four. Finally I send a single “🍑” because it is 3:29 and emojis are the last hieroglyphs we agree on. She answers with a wave, 👋, which feels permanent.  

I stand, leave the umbrella nesting in the sculpture. Maybe tomorrow someone else will inherit it, invent rain. I walk until the sidewalk ends at a chain-link fence taped with flyers for disappeared cats. Each cat has a name that took love to choose: Pixel, Chairman Meow, Desdemona. I add my notebook page by page, slipping every stuck sentence through the diamonds in the fence. The wind takes them like unpaid rent. When the book is empty it weighs nothing; I place it on the ground, cover it with a leaf the size of a child’s hand. Someone will find it, decide it’s art, Instagram it with the hashtag #unexpectedpoetry. That is also a way of being read.  

Across the street, the first train of the day departs the station without passengers, practicing. I board because the doors are open and doors are questions. Inside, the adverts have been peeled away, revealing older adverts, a geological record of desire: whitening toothpaste, boxing day sales, a musical that closed in 2014. I sit in the last car, the one that doesn’t know where it’s going. The driver is visible through plexiglass; he wears noise-canceling headphones, conducting a symphony only he can hear.  

The train accelerates. City becomes tunnel, black lens capped over the eye. My reflection appears in the window: transparent, superimposed on nothing. For the first time all night I look like someone who might forgive himself. I practice the gesture: palms open, shoulders dropped, mouth forming a vowel that has no edge.  

Lights flicker—an aneurysm of electricity—then steady.  
I understand that this is the ceremony we get instead of absolution:  
a rented carriage,  
a route that loops back on itself like a Möbius strip,  
a driver deaf to destination,  
and every seat occupied by the same passenger wearing tomorrow’s face.  

I close my eyes. The hum returns, louder now, tuned to the key of almost-dawn. It is the sound of the world being laminated, second by second, preserved under a thin plastic film. When I open them again, light is leaking into the tunnel like ink in water. The first platform appears, empty except for a janitor mopping circles that evaporate faster than he can finish them.  

The train slows. I stand. I have nothing in my hands. The doors slide apart and I step through, onto the clean side of the day, leaving the blanket of night folded on the seat behind me for whoever needs to practice dying next.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
